title: Venus Reveals a Massive Lava Tube—Bigger Than Any on Earth
summary:
Venus, often called Earth's "sister planet," is shrouded in thick clouds that hide its surface. But scientists have just uncovered a remarkable secret: a massive underground lava tube, the first ever detected on Venus. The discovery came from a fresh look at radar images taken by NASA's Magellan spacecraft in the early 1990s. The images revealed a collapsed "skylight"—a hole where the roof of a lava tube had caved in—near a broad volcano called Nyx Mons.

Lava tubes form when molten lava flows and then drains away, leaving an empty tunnel. This one is huge. The skylight is about 150 meters (490 feet) deep, and the tube itself is at least 375 meters (1,230 feet) deep. Researchers estimate it could be up to a kilometer (0.6 miles) wide, which is larger than any lava tube found on Earth or Mars. That's surprising because Venus has gravity similar to Earth's, and lower gravity usually makes it easier for large tubes to form.

"It is remarkable that we are still extracting new insights from Magellan data," says planetary scientist Anna Gülcher. The finding will influence two upcoming missions: NASA's VERITAS and the European Space Agency's EnVision, both set to launch before 2031. These spacecraft will carry advanced radar to map Venus in higher resolution.

Lava tubes on the Moon and Mars are considered potential shelters for astronauts, protecting them from radiation and meteorites. But Venus is far too extreme for human exploration. Its surface pressure is 93 times that of Earth, and temperatures are hot enough to melt electronics. Still, the discovery shows that even decades-old data can yield exciting new findings, and it deepens our understanding of volcanic activity on our mysterious neighbor.
why_it_matters: This discovery shows how reanalyzing old data can reveal new features, and it helps scientists compare volcanic processes across planets—knowledge that could shape where we look for habitable environments in the solar system.

keywords.explanation: An underground tunnel formed when molten lava flows and then drains away, leaving an empty space.
A hole where the roof of a lava tube has collapsed, revealing the tunnel beneath.
A broad volcano on Venus near which the collapsed skylight was detected.
A NASA spacecraft that mapped Venus using radar in the early 1990s.
An upcoming NASA mission to Venus that will carry advanced radar to map the surface in high resolution.
A European Space Agency mission to Venus set to launch before 2031, also with advanced radar.

questions.question: What instrument was used to discover the lava tube on Venus?
options: A camera on the International Space Station

questions.question: Approximately how deep is the skylight on Venus?
How does the size of this Venus lava tube compare to lava tubes on Earth and Mars?
options: It is larger than any found on Earth or Mars
It is smaller than those on Earth but similar to Mars
It is about the same size as the largest on Earth
questions.correct_answer: It is larger than any found on Earth or Mars

questions.question: Why is Venus considered too extreme for human exploration?
options: Surface pressure is 93 times Earth's and temperatures melt electronics
Gravity is twice that of Earth
It is too far from the Sun
questions.correct_answer: Surface pressure is 93 times Earth's and temperatures melt electronics

questions.question: Which upcoming missions will study Venus with advanced radar?
options: NASA's Magellan 2 and India's Venus Orbiter
ESA's Venus Express and Japan's Akatsuki

questions.question: What potential use do lava tubes have on the Moon and Mars?
options: Shelters for astronauts from radiation and meteorites
questions.correct_answer: Shelters for astronauts from radiation and meteorites

background_read: Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' because of similar size and gravity, but its surface is hidden by thick clouds of sulfuric acid. NASA's Magellan spacecraft (1989-1994) used synthetic aperture radar to map 98% of the surface, revealing volcanoes and strange tectonic features.

Lava tubes on the Moon and Mars are considered promising locations for future human habitats because they provide natural protection from cosmic radiation and meteorite impacts. On Earth, lava tubes exist but are smaller—the largest found is in Hawaii (around 30 meters wide).

Anna Gülcher (a planetary scientist at ETH Zürich, a top Swiss university) led the analysis of the Magellan data. Her team's work shows that even decades-old data can yield new insights, especially with modern processing techniques. The upcoming VERITAS (NASA) and EnVision (ESA) missions will carry more advanced radar to reveal Venus's geology in unprecedented detail.
Article_Structure: LEAD: first detection of a massive lava tube on Venus
└─ specific: collapsed skylight near Nyx Mons volcano
EVIDENCE: radar images from NASA's Magellan spacecraft (1990s)
├─ size: skylight 150 m deep, tube ~375 m deep, up to 1 km wide
└─ comparison: larger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars
CONTEXT: lava tubes on Moon/Mars considered astronaut shelters
└─ contrast: Venus too extreme (93x pressure, high heat)
FUTURE IMPACT: influences upcoming VERITAS and EnVision missions
└─ quote from planetary scientist Anna Gülcher
TAKEAWAY: old data still valuable, advances understanding of Venus volcanism

perspectives.description: The finding expands our knowledge of Venusian geology and proves that archived data continue to be valuable. It also provides a target for future missions to investigate Venus's volcanic history in more detail.

Venus's extreme environment means the lava tube cannot be explored directly, and the discovery doesn't directly help human exploration. Some might argue resources spent on Venus would be better used for Moon or Mars research.

The detection relies on a single collapsed skylight; the exact dimensions and continuity of the tube are uncertain. Radar images from Magellan have limited resolution, so ground truth remains elusive until newer missions arrive.

VERITAS and EnVision will provide high-resolution radar images that can identify more lava tubes and clarify their structure. This could help design future robotic missions to explore the tubes, even if humans cannot go there.
